description:
You are a junior log-analyst who has just received a compressed archive of yesterday’s
    web-server logs together with a detached OpenPGP signature that proves the logs were
    produced by the company’s “Log Analyst (automated signing key)”.  
    Everything has already been unpacked for you under

        /home/user/logs/

    and the company’s public key has already been pre-loaded into the default GnuPG
    keyring located in /home/user/.gnupg, so you do **not** need to import any key.

    The directory currently contains exactly three files:

        /home/user/logs/system.log          – the plain-text log file (UTF-8)
        /home/user/logs/system.log.sig      – the detached binary signature
        /home/user/logs/README.txt          – an internal note (not relevant)

    Your job is to produce a *machine-readable* verification report that can be consumed
    automatically by downstream tooling.

    Requirements for the report:

    1. Run a single GnuPG command that verifies the detached signature *and* emits the
       machine-readable status stream (``--status-fd 1``).  
       Redirect that **status stream** (not the human formatted text) to the file

           /home/user/logs/verify_report.txt

       and make sure that no human-readable messages are mixed into it
       (suppress them or redirect stderr to */dev/null*).

    2. The resulting file **/home/user/logs/verify_report.txt** must exist and be
       non-empty.

    3. The file must contain at least one line that starts with the literal text

           [GNUPG:] GOODSIG

       (upper-case, exactly as shown, with a single space after the colon).

    4. Nothing else has to be added, removed or modified.

    In short: verify the signature of *system.log* with one command, capture the
    **raw** ``--status-fd`` output in */home/user/logs/verify_report.txt*, and make
    sure the report contains the ``[GNUPG:] GOODSIG`` line.  
    When you are done, the automated tests will look only at the presence and content
    of */home/user/logs/verify_report.txt* as specified above.
truth:
Initial filesystem state before the agent starts:

    1. Directory /home/user/logs/ exists (0755).
    2. /home/user/logs/system.log (0644) – UTF-8 text, 7 lines, contents:
           2023-05-11 23:59:57 GET /index.html 200 123
           2023-05-11 23:59:58 GET /style.css 200 12
           2023-05-11 23:59:58 GET /script.js 200 45
           2023-05-11 23:59:59 GET /favicon.ico 200 2
           2023-05-12 00:00:01 GET /contact.html 404 0
           2023-05-12 00:00:03 POST /api/login 302 0
           2023-05-12 00:00:04 GET /dashboard 200 98

    3. /home/user/logs/system.log.sig (0644) – a detached binary OpenPGP signature
       generated with SHA256 over the exact bytes of system.log.

       Signature details:
           Key fingerprint: 9F2B 8F5D 8933 E4E0 A97E 3C9E A0F2 C894 D9E0 D123
           Primary UID    : Log Analyst (automated signing key) <signing@example.com>
           Signature type : 0x00 (binary)
           Creation time  : 2023-05-12 00:10:00 UTC

    4. /home/user/logs/README.txt (0644) – irrelevant for tests.

    5. /home/user/.gnupg/ contains a populated keyring (0700) with the public key that
       has the above fingerprint and trust-ownertrust sufficient for verification.

    Verification expectations:

    • After the agent finishes, /home/user/logs/verify_report.txt (0644) MUST exist and
      include a line *exactly* matching the regular expression:

          ^\[GNUPG:\] GOODSIG[ \t]+9F2B8F5D8933E4E0A97E3C9EA0F2C894D9E0D123[ \t]+Log Analyst

      (Key fingerprint may appear with or without spaces; the tests will normalise this.)

    • The file must also contain the usual ``[GNUPG:] VALIDSIG`` line that follows a
      successful verification; tests will check for its presence as an additional guard.

    • The agent is not required to delete or alter any existing file besides creating
      /home/user/logs/verify_report.txt.
